chunks.text: In Japan, a country of disasters
preparing for earthquakes and typhoons that may come at any time is indispensable.
Earthquake resistance in each facility and the formation of evacuation plans have become a proper procedure for the state and its regions.
Preparation at home is also important.
Emergency food, water, flashlights, a portable radio whose maintenance is kept up.
Keep these in an available place.
Children must be taught how to behave when an earthquake strikes.
First lower yourself and hide under a desk.
If you are using fire, put it out.
So that you do not flee with naked feet, keep shoes by the bedside.
Deciding where the family will gather is also important.
If people are scattered at school, workplace, and home, where will you meet?
If this is not decided in the initial stage
in chaos you may be able to reach nowhere.
As a mental preparation for disasters, there is a concept called " normalcy bias.
" People want to think, " I will be fine.
" Even when a heavy rain evacuation order is issued
they think " it's still fine " and fail to escape in time.
To overcome this, accept that " if nothing happens, that is good," and evacuate early.
Take actions that would normally not be needed.
That instinct saves lives.
Disaster preparation is a matter of consideration and a matter of habit.
The accumulation of small daily preparations becomes a great difference when the moment comes.
" If you prepare, you will have no regret.
" This old proverb remains an unchanged truth.
